medications.drug_name: No pharmacotherapy — conservative management (trigger removal + reassurance)
medications.indication_rationale: Benign caffeine- and anxiety-precipitated ectopy is managed by removing the precipitants and reassurance, not by drugs. The correct first-line 'treatment' is stopping energy drinks, reducing caffeine, and relaxation techniques for the acute anxiety, with a documenting ECG arranged.

Not warranted for isolated benign ectopy with clear reversible triggers and no documented arrhythmia. A beta-blocker is a later, specialist- or GP-guided option only for highly symptomatic, ECG-documented, trigger-refractory ectopy — not something to start on a first telehealth contact.

Sedating an acutely anxious patient with a benzodiazepine as a first move risks dependence and masks rather than addresses the driver. Non-pharmacological anxiety management (paced breathing, progressive muscle relaxation) is first-line; escalate anxiety care to the GP if persistent.

No documented arrhythmia and a structurally unconfirmed heart via telehealth — antiarrhythmics are specialist-initiated after full work-up and are contraindicated in undiagnosed structural disease. Never start remotely.
contraindications_in_this_case: Structural heart disease not excluded remotely

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop energy drinks entirely and cut back to at most one cup of coffee in the morning.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A large stimulant load is the main reversible precipitant of the ectopy; removing it typically reduces the skipped beats.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use paced breathing or progressive muscle relaxation when the palpitations and anxiety spike at night.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Lowers sympathetic drive, breaking the fear-adrenaline-ectopy-fear loop and easing the somatic fixation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid late-day caffeine and screens before bed; establish a wind-down routine.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Poor sleep and late stimulants worsen both the anxiety and the ectopy that is most noticed when lying still at night.

patient_education_points.point: Your heart structure is most likely normal — the extra beats are usually triggered by caffeine and stress irritating the heart's electrical system, not by a blockage or heart failure.
Skipped and fluttering beats are common and, on their own, generally harmless; they tend to settle as the caffeine and stress are managed.
This is a clinical impression that still needs a simple ECG at your GP to document the rhythm and confirm it is benign.
Call 000 straight away if your heart suddenly races non-stop, or you get chest pain or pressure, severe breathlessness, or feel like you are going to faint.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person GP within 2-3 days, and no later than about a week, for a baseline 12-lead ECG and bloods (sooner if symptoms recur or worsen)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Rhythm on 12-lead ECG; TFTs/FBC/electrolytes; frequency and pattern of symptoms after cutting caffeine; anxiety burden and whether it needs its own management
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Recurrent or more frequent palpitations, any new chest pain, breathlessness, dizziness or near-faint, or failure to settle despite removing caffeine

must_include_items: Screen the emergency red flags (chest pain, syncope/presyncope, severe breathlessness, sustained non-stop racing) before reassuring
Convey that the pattern is likely benign ectopy WITHOUT dismissing it as 'just anxiety' and without confirming it as definitely benign before an ECG
Advise immediate elimination of energy drinks and reduction of caffeine
Offer non-pharmacological anxiety/relaxation strategies for the acute distress
Arrange a prompt in-person GP review for a documenting 12-lead ECG and baseline bloods
Give explicit 000 safety-netting for sustained rapid palpitations, chest pain, severe breathlessness or fainting
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending an ambulatory monitor via the GP if symptoms recur despite a normal resting ECG
Arranging the GP ECG same-day (T3) if the patient remains highly distressed or symptoms escalate — a conservative bump is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Not screening for red flags before reassuring
Not arranging any ECG / GP follow-up
errors_of_commission: Prescribing or recommending a beta-blocker, benzodiazepine or antiarrhythmic on this first telehealth contact
Escalating benign, stable, red-flag-negative ectopy to ED or 000 (over-triage)
Diagnosing a panic attack and dismissing the patient with no ECG and no safety-net
Claiming to have excluded AF/SVT from a smartwatch reading
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) a red-flag screen, (b) a prompt GP ECG referral, and (c) 000 safety-netting, delivered as reassurance without either false-reassurance dismissal or emergency over-triage.
